stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \), with domain all real numbers. What is \( f([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Compare \( [[x0]] \) with the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) to pick the correct piece of the domain, then substitute. This gives \( f([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the rule for \( x < [[t]] \); check which side of \( [[t]] \) the input \( [[x0]] \) is on.
This uses the rule for \( x \ge [[t]] \); check which side of \( [[t]] \) the input \( [[x0]] \) is on.
This substitutes the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) into the lower piece instead of the input \( [[x0]] \).
This substitutes the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) into the upper piece instead of the input \( [[x0]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x \le [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x > [[t]] \end{cases} \), with domain all real numbers. What is \( f([[t]]) \)?
solution: The breakpoint \( x=[[t]] \) satisfies \( x \le [[t]] \) (not strict), so the FIRST piece applies: \( f([[t]]) = [[a]]([[t]]) + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the second piece, but the condition \( x \le [[t]] \) on the first piece includes the breakpoint itself.
This flips the sign of the constant term.
This uses the wrong piece and also flips the sign of its constant term.
This reports the input value itself instead of evaluating \( f \) at it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{[[m]]x + ([[e]])} & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \). Exactly one real number must be excluded from the domain of \( f \), because it makes a denominator zero. What is that excluded number?
solution: The first piece is undefined exactly when its denominator vanishes: \( [[m]]x + ([[e]]) = 0 \), so \( x = [[answer]] \). (The constraint that this value lies below [[t]] guarantees it truly falls inside the first piece's own domain restriction, so it really is excluded.)
distractor_rationales: This is the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) itself, not the value that zeroes the denominator.
This flips the sign when solving \( [[m]]x+([[e]])=0 \) for \( x \).
This inverts the ratio of the coefficients instead of solving for \( x \).
This inverts the ratio and flips its sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[a]]x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \). The vertex of the parabola piece lies within its domain restriction \( x \ge [[t]] \). What is the minimum value attained by \( f \) on that piece?
solution: The parabola \( [[a]]x^{2}+([[B]])x+([[C]]) \) has its vertex at \( x = -\dfrac{[[B]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} = [[h]] \), which satisfies \( x \ge [[t]] \), so the minimum on this piece is the vertex's \( y \)-value: \( [[C]] - \dfrac{([[B]])^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the constant term \( C \) directly instead of completing the square to find the vertex value.
This reports only the correction term, dropping the constant \( C \).
This adds the correction term instead of subtracting it.
This reports the vertex's \( x \)-coordinate instead of the minimum \( y \)-value.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.

solution: Locate \( [[x0]] \) among the three intervals of the domain determined by the breakpoints \( [[t1]] \) and \( [[t2]] \), then apply that piece's rule. This gives \( f([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).

This uses the middle piece's rule; check which of the three domain intervals \( [[x0]] \) actually falls in.
This uses the last piece's rule; check which of the three domain intervals \( [[x0]] \) actually falls in.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \). Given that the value \( V=[[V]] \) is attained on the \( x \ge [[t]] \) branch of the domain, which of the following is a solution of \( f(x) = [[V]] \)?
solution: Since we are told \( V \) is attained on the \( x \ge [[t]] \) branch, solve that branch's equation: \( [[c]]x + ([[d]]) = [[V]] \), giving \( x = [[answer]] \).

This incorrectly solves the OTHER branch's equation, which does not apply here.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).
